text: An acute dose of 70 ml nitrate-rich beetroot juice (~4.1 mmol nitrate) taken 2.5 hours before a 5-km time trial does not improve performance compared to placebo in recreational runners.
The difference in 5-km time between beetroot juice and placebo conditions was negligible at 0.8 seconds with a zero effect size (d = 0.00).

Both beetroot juice and placebo produced similar ~22-second improvements over baseline in competitive 5-km time trials, suggesting the performance benefit is driven by expectancy or competitive context rather than nitrate supplementation.

Placebo effects in competitive running settings can produce meaningful performance improvements (~22 seconds over 5 km), highlighting the importance of placebo-controlled designs when evaluating supplements.
A single acute dose of ~4.1 mmol nitrate from beetroot juice may be insufficient to produce ergogenic effects; higher doses (≥6.4 mmol) have shown benefits in other studies.
